name: Lithium
name_origin: From Greek 'lithos' (stone), because it was first identified in a mineral (petalite), in contrast to sodium and potassium which were found in plant ashes.

occurrence.abundance_summary: Lithium is the lightest metal and the lightest solid element, present at ~20 ppm in Earth's crust. It is never found as a free element in nature; commercial sources include continental brines (Chile, Argentina, China) and pegmatite ores such as spodumene (Australia).
states: dissolved in continental brines and salars

compounds.name: Lithium carbonate
compounds.summary: A white salt used as a precursor for battery-grade lithium chemicals and as a mood-stabilizing pharmaceutical.

compounds.name: Lithium hydroxide
compounds.summary: A strong base used in lithium-ion battery cathode synthesis and as a CO2 scrubber on spacecraft.

compounds.name: Lithium chloride
compounds.summary: A hygroscopic salt used in desiccants, brazing fluxes, and as an electrolyte in molten-salt processes.

compounds.name: Lithium hydride
compounds.summary: A saline hydride used as a hydrogen source and as a neutron shielding material.

compounds.name: Lithium aluminum hydride
compounds.summary: A powerful reducing agent widely used in organic synthesis to reduce esters, amides, and carboxylic acids.

uses: Cathode and anode materials for rechargeable lithium-ion batteries
Mood-stabilizing medication for bipolar disorder (lithium carbonate, lithium citrate)
Heat-resistant glass and ceramics (lithium oxide flux)
Aluminum-lithium alloys for aerospace structural components
Tritium production and fusion fuel (6Li in breeding blankets)
reactivity_summary: Lithium is a soft, silvery alkali metal that tarnishes rapidly in air, forming a dark oxide-nitride coating. It reacts with water to produce hydrogen and lithium hydroxide, but less violently than sodium or potassium. Uniquely among the alkali metals, it reacts directly with nitrogen at room temperature to form Li3N.
isotopes_summary: Lithium has two stable isotopes, 7Li (~92.4%) and 6Li (~7.6%); the natural ratio varies enough that IUPAC reports the atomic weight as an interval. 6Li is critical to thermonuclear weapons and proposed fusion reactors as a tritium-breeding material.

citations.source: CRC Handbook of Chemistry and Physics, 102nd ed.
Royal Society of Chemistry - Periodic Table: Lithium
